pmos

Product Roadmap, Prioritization & Goal Setting — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Strategy. Roadmapping and prioritization are where strategy either becomes execution or quietly dies, and the canon here is contentious in a way the strategy canon is not. The roadmap camp splits cleanly: Marty Cagan and Teresa Torres treat the feature-list-on-a-timeline as the single biggest source of waste in product, while Janna Bastow (Now-Next-Later) and Roman Pichler (the GO outcome roadmap) offer the formats that replace it. The prioritization frameworks line up by what they optimize: RICE for throughput, the Kano model for satisfaction type, WSJF and Cost of Delay for economic sequencing, opportunity scoring and Ulwick's ODI for under-served needs, and MoSCoW for fast scoping. Goal-setting is anchored by Doerr's OKRs (from Grove at Intel), with Christina Wodtke on focus and Felipe Castro on the outcomes-vs-output discipline that keeps OKRs honest. Where the camps disagree — dates on roadmaps, whether roadmaps help at all, OKR theater, the false precision of scoring — this primer flags it rather than resolving it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 15 verified sources.

What a roadmap is actually for

The roadmap is the most fought-over artifact in product, and the fight is really about a single confusion: is a roadmap a delivery commitment or a communication tool? The two loudest voices in the canon agree on the diagnosis and differ only in tone. Marty Cagan's position is deliberately measured — "Used properly roadmaps are a helpful tool, the problem is almost no-one uses them that way" — before he delivers the indictment: "95% of roadmaps are not outcome-based roadmaps. They're output roadmaps," and "in feature team companies, roadmaps are usually the biggest single source of waste." The waste is not the document; it is what the document encodes. A list of features with dates is a list of bets the organization has pre-committed to before learning whether any of them solve the problem.

Teresa Torres is blunter still: "Feature-based roadmaps are fiction. Everyone on the product team knows it," because "nobody can predict the future" and "the further away something is, the more uncertain it is." Her reframe is the most useful single idea in the roadmap literature — a roadmap should mirror the actual shape of the team's knowledge: "specific in the near term, directional in the long term." The unit that survives contact with reality is the problem, not the feature: "The solution changed, but the opportunity we're addressing hasn't." When a team ships something other than the named feature, a feature roadmap reads as a broken promise; an opportunity roadmap reads as the system working as designed.

Cagan's constructive half is about sequencing rather than abolition: in a healthy product organization "the roadmap is the communication tool once you actually know what you need to do," and teams are "given problems to solve," not features to build. The throughline across both camps is that the roadmap's legitimate job is to align stakeholders on direction and intent under acknowledged uncertainty — and that the moment it hardens into a dated feature contract, it stops describing reality and starts manufacturing the waste Cagan names.

Further reading: svpg.com — Marty Cagan argues product roadmaps should be simple, high-level paths from current state to product vision — not prioritized feature-request lists — and warns that feature-driven roadmaps skip the critical work of product discovery.

Roadmap formats — from timelines to horizons

If the timeline roadmap is the problem, the canon's answer is to ration certainty by horizon rather than pretending to forecast it. The dominant format is Janna Bastow's Now-Next-Later, which she built explicitly as a reaction: "The Now-Next-Later roadmap exists because timeline roadmaps aren't effective, simple as that," and "Deadlines are the root cause of so much product management pain." The structural insight is that the three columns carry different epistemic weight — "The Now column is definite, while the Later column is full of possibilities" — so the format builds uncertainty into its grammar. Later items are allowed to be vague because they are supposed to be; the format "gives you the chance to become more certain, rather than commit too early" and keeps teams "focused on discovery."

The unavoidable question with any horizon format is whether to attach dates at all, and the most-cited nuanced answer is Roman Pichler's, which turns on audience. For an externally facing roadmap "I recommend that you do NOT state any dates," because a date shown to a customer becomes a promise. But for "an internal product roadmap that guides the work of stakeholders and development teams, I recommend using specific time frames or dates" — internal teams need coordination anchors that customers should never see. Where dates appear, Pichler argues for coarse ranges — "big, coarse-grained time frames like 'in the first half of the year and the second half of the year'" — or Bastow's horizons outright, to avoid "putting yourself and your development teams under pressure and disappointing customers." The format choice, in other words, is downstream of who is reading it.

Further reading: svpg.com — Marty Cagan argues product roadmaps should be simple, high-level paths from current state to product vision — not prioritized feature-request lists — and warns that feature-driven roadmaps skip the critical work of product discovery.

Outcome-based roadmaps

The format that most directly answers Cagan's "output roadmap" critique is the outcome-based roadmap, and Roman Pichler draws the contrast as cleanly as anyone: "A traditional roadmap is essentially a list of features, which are mapped onto a timeline," whereas an outcome-based roadmap centers "the specific value the product should create — the outcomes it should achieve." The mental shift is from what to why: "Instead of focussing on what needs to be delivered, you ask why it is worthwhile progressing the product." Outcomes in his sense are concrete and measurable product effects — acquiring new users, reducing churn, increasing engagement — not vague aspirations.

Pichler's GO Product Roadmap is the canonical template for this. It "places the outcomes at the centre," organizing the roadmap around goals, and treats features as subordinate evidence rather than the headline: features are kept "coarse-grained" and deliberately "limited to three to five capabilities per goal." That constraint is the whole point. By capping features per goal, the format forces the team to argue that a small set of bets will move the named outcome, rather than smuggling a long feature backlog in under a thin outcome label. An outcome roadmap with twenty features per goal has quietly reverted to a feature roadmap with a nicer cover page.

The practical tension every team hits is that stakeholders often want the features back — they find outcomes abstract and features reassuringly concrete. The canon's answer is not to capitulate but to keep the features present and subordinate: the goal states the outcome and the why, and the three-to-five capabilities show enough of the how to be credible without re-committing the team to a fixed solution. The outcome roadmap is the bridge between strategy and the prioritization frameworks below, because it is the artifact that says which outcomes are in play this period — and therefore which prioritization question the team is even asking.

Further reading: svpg.com — Marty Cagan argues product roadmaps should be simple, high-level paths from current state to product vision — not prioritized feature-request lists — and warns that feature-driven roadmaps skip the critical work of product discovery.

RICE — scoring for throughput

Once the outcomes are set, prioritization is the work of ordering the bets, and RICE is the most widely adopted scoring model for it. As Product School documents, RICE was "Developed by Sean McBride at Intercom" and ranks initiatives by "(Reach * Impact * Confidence) / Effort." The four factors are deliberately mundane: Reach "measures the number of people or users affected by a project within a specific time frame"; Impact "assesses the significance of the project's effect"; Confidence "reflects how certain you are about your estimates," expressed as a percentage; and Effort "measures the amount of work required."

The design intelligence of RICE is in the two factors teams most want to skip. Confidence is an explicit discount on wishful thinking — a high-reach, high-impact idea with a 50% confidence score is automatically halved, which is exactly the correction an enthusiastic champion needs. Effort sits alone in the denominator, so RICE rewards leverage: a modest-impact, trivial-effort change can outrank a glamorous, expensive one. That is the throughput bias built into the formula. RICE is asking "what gets us the most validated value per unit of work," which is the right question for a team trying to maximize the number of good bets it can run.

The honest limitation, which practitioners surface constantly, is precision theater. The output is a single number to a decimal place built from four estimates, several of them guesses, and the apparent precision can launder a weak input into a confident ranking. The disciplined use treats RICE as a structured conversation — a forcing function to make reach, confidence, and effort explicit and contestable — rather than an oracle whose decimal ordering is taken literally. Used that way it is a defense against the loudest-voice-wins failure mode; used literally it manufactures false certainty, a tension the live-debates section returns to.

The Kano model — types of satisfaction

RICE ranks features against each other on a single axis of value-per-effort; the Kano model does something orthogonal and complementary — it asks what kind of satisfaction a feature produces, which determines how its value behaves as you invest in it. Per ProductPlan's reference, the model (from Noriaki Kano in the 1980s) prioritizes features "based on the degree to which they are likely to satisfy customers," and sorts them into categories that respond very differently to investment.

The three load-bearing categories are basic, performance, and excitement. Basic or threshold features are expected and taken for granted — the canonical example is "a car's turn signal" — so their presence buys no delight while their absence causes real dissatisfaction; you must do them and get no credit. Performance (or one-dimensional) features deliver satisfaction "in proportion" to how much you invest, the linear middle of the model where more is straightforwardly better. Excitement or attractive features create "dramatic customer delight" when present, yet "customers might not even miss them" when absent — these are the asymmetric upside bets. A fourth category, indifferent, captures features customers "won't care about" no matter how much effort you pour in, which is the most useful warning the model issues.

The strategic payoff is that the categories prescribe different investment logics. Basic features are table stakes — fund them to parity and stop, because over-investing buys nothing. Performance features are where competitive positioning is fought on a linear curve. Excitement features are the differentiators worth disproportionate bets, with the crucial caveat the canon always attaches: today's delighter decays into tomorrow's expected baseline as the market catches up, so the excitement category is a moving target that has to be continually refreshed. Kano does not rank features; it tells you what game each feature is playing, which is the input a RICE score cannot supply.

Cost of Delay and WSJF — economic sequencing

RICE and Kano are largely silent on time, and time is exactly where the next school concentrates. Cost of Delay reframes prioritization as an economic question about when. As Black Swan Farming puts it, "Cost of Delay is a way of communicating the impact of time on the outcomes we hope to achieve" — Don Reinertsen calls it "the golden key that unlocks many doors... an astonishing power to transform the mindset of a development organisation." Its specific power is that it "combines urgency and value — two things that humans are not very good at distinguishing between." A feature can be highly valuable yet not urgent, or modest in value but rapidly decaying; Cost of Delay forces those two dimensions apart rather than letting "important" blur them together.

The operational expression of Cost of Delay is Weighted Shortest Job First. Per the same source, WSJF works by "dividing the estimated Cost of Delay by the estimated duration" — and "the weighting that Don specifically recommends for product development is Cost of Delay" because most organizations "are most interested in maximising their economic return." Putting duration in the denominator "optimises the return on investment by minimising the total Delay Cost incurred," and it carries a useful side effect: it "has the benefit of encouraging the breakdown of work into smaller batches," since shorter jobs with the same Cost of Delay rank higher. WSJF therefore rewards exactly the small-batch behavior that lean product development wants anyway.

Where RICE optimizes value-per-effort, WSJF optimizes value-decay-per-duration — a subtly but importantly different objective. The two can disagree: a high-RICE feature whose value is stable over time may rank below a lower-RICE feature whose value evaporates if delayed a quarter. WSJF is the framework for portfolios where timing is the dominant risk — regulatory deadlines, market windows, competitive responses — and it is the most economically literate of the scoring models, at the cost of demanding Cost-of-Delay estimates that are genuinely hard to produce.

Opportunity scoring and outcome-driven innovation

Every framework so far scores solutions the team has already imagined. Opportunity scoring inverts the input: it scores needs, surfacing where to aim before any feature exists. As ProductPlan describes, it is "an importance-versus-satisfaction analysis" traceable to "the outcome-driven-innovation (ODI) method created by business consultant Tony Ulwick in the 1990s." The algorithm is "Importance + (importance – satisfaction) = opportunity," and the targets it surfaces are the needs that "receive the highest aggregate 'importance' and lowest aggregate 'satisfaction' scores" — what customers care about most and are served worst on.

The underlying theory is Ulwick's Outcome-Driven Innovation, which sits squarely in the jobs-to-be-done tradition: "People buy products and services to get jobs done," and "as people complete these jobs, they have certain measurable outcomes that they are attempting to achieve." ODI "attempts to identify jobs and outcomes that are either important but poorly served or unimportant but over-served," using the same "Importance + (Importance-Satisfaction) = Opportunity" formula — a construction that deliberately "gives twice as much weight to importance as to satisfaction." The doubling matters: it biases the model toward needs customers genuinely care about, preventing it from chasing low-stakes dissatisfactions that are easy to fix but not worth fixing.

The two halves of Ulwick's framing — underserved and overserved — are each strategically loaded. Underserved outcomes are the white space where new value lives; overserved outcomes are where the market has gilded the lily and a cheaper, simpler entrant can disrupt from below. Opportunity scoring is the prioritization framework most tightly coupled to discovery, because its inputs are research findings about importance and satisfaction rather than internal guesses about reach and effort. It tells a team where the prize is; RICE and WSJF then sequence the bets aimed at it.

Lightweight frameworks — MoSCoW and the speed tradeoff

The scoring models above buy rigor with overhead — research inputs, estimation sessions, spreadsheets. A second family trades that rigor for speed, and the most durable example is MoSCoW. Per airfocus, MoSCoW sorts work into "four prioritization categories: Must-have, Should-have, Could-have, and Won't-have." Must-haves are "the minimum requirements... non-negotiable"; Should-haves "add substantial value" but are not essential; Could-haves are "neat, interesting, or fun, but... [don't] necessarily serve any greater purpose"; and Won't-haves are explicitly deferred this round. The method came from "Dai Clegg... consulting on software development at Oracle" in 1994 and "became popular in the agile project delivery framework DSDM."

The quietly important category is Won't-have. Most lightweight prioritization fails not because teams cannot identify Must-haves but because nothing is ever explicitly excluded, so scope creeps under the cover of "we'll get to it." MoSCoW makes exclusion a named, first-class decision, which is most of its practical value. The cost of that simplicity is that the buckets are coarse and gameable — everything drifts toward Must-have under stakeholder pressure, and the framework offers no internal mechanism to resist that drift the way RICE's effort denominator or WSJF's duration term do.

The honest framing of the lightweight family is that it optimizes for decision speed and shared understanding over analytical defensibility. MoSCoW is ideal for time-boxed scoping — a release, a sprint, a fixed-deadline launch — where a coarse, fast, communicable sort beats a precise one that arrives too late. It is a poor fit for genuinely close calls across a large portfolio, where the discriminating power of a numeric model earns its overhead. The skill is matching the framework's rigor to the decision's stakes, not adopting one framework for everything.

Further reading: blog.brianbalfour.com — Everything's changed.

OKRs and goal-setting

Prioritization frameworks order bets; goal-setting frameworks define what winning means, and OKRs are the dominant grammar for it. The canonical definition is John Doerr's formula: "I will (Objective) as measured by (Key Results)." The Objective is "what is to be achieved, no more and no less"; the Key Results "benchmark and monitor how we get to the Objective," and the standard structure pairs "an Objective at the top and 3-5 supporting Key Results below it." The lineage is worth knowing because it explains the method's spirit: "The OKR methodology was created by Andy Grove at Intel and taught to John Doerr by him," and Grove "rarely mentioned Objectives without tying them to 'Key Results,' a term he seems to have coined himself."

The discipline OKRs impose is the forced separation of aspiration from evidence. The Objective is allowed to be qualitative and motivating; the Key Results must be measurable, so a team cannot declare victory on vibes. Christina Wodtke, whose Radical Focus is the practitioner companion to Doerr, treats OKRs primarily as a focus instrument: "You don't pour whiskey through a leaky funnel into a flask and hope to get drunk; you don't want a dozen Objectives for the same reason." Her planning posture — the "Half-Built Strategy" — argues for "just enough planning to know what to do and what to watch for, but not so much you are locked into a bad game plan." She also names the most common practical failure: "Setting Key Results takes quite a bit of time," and is harder than writing the Objective, which is why teams under-invest in the part that actually does the work.

Focus is the recurring theme across the OKR canon. The frameworks all push toward fewer Objectives than feels comfortable, because the entire mechanism is sacrifice — an OKR set that includes everything important has communicated nothing about what to drop when time runs short. Wodtke's leaky-funnel image is the canon's most memorable statement of the point: the value of OKRs is not in the goals you list but in the goals you were forced to leave off.

Further reading: amazon.com — Amazon audiobook listing for Measure What Matters by John Doerr, which covers the OKR goal-setting framework as adopted by Google and other leading organizations.

Connecting OKRs to the roadmap

OKRs and outcome roadmaps are the same idea expressed in two artifacts, and the connective tissue is the distinction between outcomes and output. Felipe Castro's central rule is the test that keeps the whole system honest: "Good Key Results measure outcomes: the value and the benefits you deliver to your customers or your company," and "A major benefit of OKR is moving from project management to outcomes thinking." His diagnostic cuts straight through the most common failure: "If you deliver all your tasks and nothing improves, are you still successful? Of course not." A Key Result that counts shipped features is a disguised output roadmap; a Key Result that measures the change those features were supposed to cause is the genuine article.

This is where goal-setting and roadmapping fuse. Pichler's outcome roadmap names the outcomes for the period; well-formed OKRs measure whether those outcomes are moving; and the prioritization frameworks order the bets the team will make to move them. Run correctly, the chain is unbroken — strategy sets the diagnosis, the outcome roadmap selects this period's outcomes, the OKRs make them measurable, and RICE or WSJF sequences the work. Run incorrectly, each layer quietly reverts to output: the roadmap lists features, the Key Results count those features, and the team is busy and unaccountable.

Castro also imports Doerr's most counterintuitive governance rule — the "amicable divorce between OKRs and compensation," keeping goal-setting and performance pay as "two distinct conversations." The logic is that the moment Key Results determine bonuses, people set Key Results they are certain to hit, which destroys the ambition the framework exists to create. This is the single most violated OKR rule in practice, and the most consequential: a system designed for honest stretch goals, wired to pay, predictably produces sandbagged ones.

Managing the roadmap over time

A roadmap is a verb more than a noun — its value is in how it is maintained, not how it is first drawn. The canon's central operating discipline is the one Torres names: a roadmap is a standing relationship with stakeholders, and "When the product team breaks the contract... trust erodes." The contract is not "we will ship exactly these features"; with an opportunity-shaped roadmap the contract is "we will keep pursuing these problems and tell you honestly what we learn." That contract survives a pivot precisely because, as Torres puts it, "The solution changed, but the opportunity we're addressing hasn't" — the commitment was to the problem, so changing the solution honors the contract rather than breaking it.

The cadence question — how often to revisit — is answered by treating the strategy as living. The near horizon firms up as discovery completes and the far horizon stays deliberately loose, so maintenance is mostly the work of promoting items from Later to Next to Now as certainty accrues, and demoting or dropping the bets that discovery kills. Bastow's format makes this maintenance natural: because Later is explicitly "full of possibilities," removing something from it costs no credibility, whereas removing a dated feature from a timeline roadmap reads as failure.

The dates question recurs here as an ongoing governance choice rather than a one-time format decision. Pichler's internal-versus-external split is the maintenance rule, not just the authoring rule: the internal roadmap can carry coarse time frames for coordination and gets revised openly as those frames shift, while the external roadmap stays date-free so that revision is invisible to customers and never reads as a missed promise. Managing the roadmap well is mostly the discipline of keeping those two versions distinct and resisting the pressure to leak internal dates outward.

Choosing an approach

The frameworks are not competitors; they answer different questions, and the practitioner skill is diagnosing which question is live. The roadmap format question is settled mostly by audience and uncertainty: high-uncertainty, discovery-driven work wants Bastow's horizons or Pichler's outcome roadmap, and the date decision follows Pichler's internal-versus-external split rather than a blanket rule. There is no version of the canon in which a dated feature timeline shown to customers is the recommended default.

The prioritization framework question is settled by what is scarce and what is uncertain. When throughput is the constraint and you are choosing among imagined solutions, RICE's value-per-effort is the right lens. When timing dominates — windows, deadlines, decaying value — WSJF and Cost of Delay are the economically literate choice. When you do not yet know what to build, opportunity scoring and ODI point at underserved needs before any feature exists. When a feature's value behavior is the question, Kano classifies it. And when speed of decision matters more than analytical defensibility — time-boxed scoping under a deadline — MoSCoW's coarse, fast sort wins. Most mature teams run two or three of these for different decisions rather than standardizing on one, because standardizing forces every decision through a lens built for a different question.

Goal-setting is the layer that ties the rest to strategy. OKRs are the dominant grammar, but the canon is unanimous that their value is conditional on the outcomes-not-output discipline Castro names and the focus discipline Wodtke insists on — an OKR system that counts features and pays bonuses on Key Results has reproduced the exact output-roadmap pathology Cagan indicts, just in a new artifact. The frameworks are tools; the judgment is in matching each tool to the decision in front of you and refusing to let any one of them become the answer to questions it was never built to ask.

Live debates — where practitioners disagree

Several of these questions are genuinely contested, and a primer that presented any of them as settled would be misleading; here the disagreement is the point.

Do roadmaps help or harm? This is the sharpest live debate. Cagan calls roadmaps in feature-team companies "usually the biggest single source of waste" and Torres calls feature roadmaps "fiction," yet neither abolishes the artifact — Cagan explicitly says "Used properly roadmaps are a helpful tool." The disagreement in practice is about whether the typical organization can use them properly, with skeptics arguing the failure mode is so dominant that the artifact does net harm and pragmatists arguing the outcome-based reform is achievable. This is contested and unresolved.

Should roadmaps carry dates? Pichler's internal-versus-external split is the canonical reconciliation, but it is not universally accepted — some practitioners hold that any date, even internal, recreates the deadline pathology Bastow built Now-Next-Later to escape, while others argue that engineering and go-to-market simply cannot coordinate without time anchors. Practitioners disagree on where the line sits.

OKR theater and the compensation question. The outcomes-vs-output failure Castro names is widely acknowledged, but the fix is contested. Doerr and Castro insist on divorcing OKRs from compensation; many organizations find that divorce culturally impossible and wire goals to pay anyway, which the canon predicts will produce sandbagged targets. Whether OKRs can survive in a high-accountability comp culture at all is an open and actively argued question.

The false precision of scoring. RICE, WSJF, and opportunity scoring all emit numbers built from estimates, and practitioners split on whether the numeric output is a useful forcing function or a dangerous laundering of guesses into apparent certainty. The defensible middle — treat scores as structured conversation, not oracle — is widely stated but unevenly practiced, and the critique that scoring frameworks mostly manufacture confidence remains live.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

This primer sits at the execution edge of product strategy; the threads below are where it connects to the rest of the discipline.

  • Product strategy & vision — the upstream layer that sets the diagnosis these roadmaps and goals execute against; Rumelt's kernel and the vision/strategy/roadmap hierarchy are the prerequisites for everything here.
  • Continuous discovery — the practice that feeds opportunity scoring and ODI their inputs and that turns Later-column possibilities into validated Now-column commitments.
  • Outcomes vs. outputs & metrics — the measurement discipline (North Star, leading vs. lagging indicators) that makes outcome roadmaps and Key Results honest rather than decorative.
  • Stakeholder management & communication — the relationship work behind Torres's roadmap-as-contract and Pichler's internal-versus-external split.
  • Agile & delivery frameworks — where MoSCoW, WSJF, and the small-batch bias originate, and where the roadmap meets the backlog.
  • Jobs-to-be-done — the theory under Ulwick's ODI and the deeper frame for what "importance" and "satisfaction" are measuring.

References

  1. talkingroadmaps.com ·cited· (T1)

    Marty Cagan's position is that roadmaps are a tool, not the enemy: "Used properly roadmaps are a helpful tool, the problem is almost no-one uses them that way." His indictment is sharp — "95% of roadmaps are not outcome-based roadmaps. They're output roadmaps" — and "in feature team companies, roadmaps are usually the biggest single source of waste." The fix is sequencing: "in a good product company, the roadmap is the communication tool once you actually know what you need to do," and teams are "given problems to solve" not features to build.

  2. producttalk.org ·cited· (T1)

    Teresa Torres argues feature roadmaps are dishonest about uncertainty: "Feature-based roadmaps are fiction. Everyone on the product team knows it," because "nobody can predict the future" and "the further away something is, the more uncertain it is." A good roadmap mirrors the actual state of knowledge — "specific in the near term, directional in the long term" — and the unit that survives a pivot is the problem, not the feature: "The solution changed, but the opportunity we're addressing hasn't." Breaking the implied delivery contract erodes stakeholder trust.

  3. prodpad.com ·cited· (T1)

    Janna Bastow invented the Now-Next-Later roadmap as a reaction against timeline/Gantt roadmaps: "The Now-Next-Later roadmap exists because timeline roadmaps aren't effective, simple as that" and "Deadlines are the root cause of so much product management pain." Its core idea is to ration certainty by horizon — "The Now column is definite, while the Later column is full of possibilities" — which "gives you the chance to become more certain, rather than commit too early" and keeps teams "focused on discovery" rather than deadline-based delivery.

  4. productschool.com ·cited· (T1)

    RICE, "Developed by Sean McBride at Intercom," scores initiatives on four factors and ranks by "(Reach * Impact * Confidence) / Effort." Reach "measures the number of people or users affected by a project within a specific time frame"; Impact "assesses the significance of the project's effect"; Confidence "reflects how certain you are about your estimates" (a percentage that discounts wishful scoring); Effort "measures the amount of work required." Its discipline is forcing reach, confidence, and effort into the decision rather than championing pet ideas.

  5. whatmatters.com ·cited· (T1)

    John Doerr's OKR formula is "I will (Objective) as measured by (Key Results)." The Objective is "what is to be achieved, no more and no less"; Key Results "benchmark and monitor how we get to the Objective," written "with an Objective at the top and 3-5 supporting Key Results below it." The lineage: "The OKR methodology was created by Andy Grove at Intel and taught to John Doerr by him" — Grove "rarely mentioned Objectives without tying them to 'Key Results,' a term he seems to have coined himself."

  6. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (T1)

    Christina Wodtke (Radical Focus) treats OKRs as forced focus and just-enough planning: "You don't pour whiskey through a leaky funnel into a flask and hope to get drunk; you don't want a dozen Objectives for the same reason." Her planning posture is the "Half-Built Strategy" — "You want just enough planning to know what to do and what to watch for, but not so much you are locked into a bad game plan" — and she warns that "Setting Key Results takes quite a bit of time," harder than writing the Objective itself.

  7. medium.com ·cited· (T1)

    Felipe Castro's central rule: "Good Key Results measure outcomes: the value and the benefits you deliver to your customers or your company," and "A major benefit of OKR is moving from project management to outcomes thinking." His test cuts through OKR theater — "If you deliver all your tasks and nothing improves, are you still successful? Of course not" — and he reinforces Doerr's "amicable divorce between OKRs and compensation," keeping goal-setting and performance pay as "two distinct conversations."

  8. romanpichler.medium.com ·cited· (T2)

    Roman Pichler's nuanced answer on dates: for an externally-facing roadmap "I recommend that you do NOT state any dates," but for "an internal product roadmap that guides the work of stakeholders and development teams, I recommend using specific time frames or dates." Where dates are used, prefer coarse ranges — "big, coarse-grained time frames like 'in the first half of the year and the second half of the year'" or Bastow's now-next-later — to avoid "putting yourself and your development teams under pressure and disappointing customers."

  9. romanpichler.medium.com ·cited· (T2)

    Roman Pichler contrasts the two roadmap species: "A traditional roadmap is essentially a list of features, which are mapped onto a timeline," while an outcome-based roadmap focuses on "the specific value the product should create — the outcomes it should achieve." The shift is from what to why: "Instead of focussing on what needs to be delivered, you ask why it is worthwhile progressing the product" (acquiring users, reducing churn, increasing engagement). His GO Product Roadmap "places the outcomes at the centre," with features kept "coarse-grained" and "limited to three to five capabilities per goal."

  10. productplan.com ·cited· (T2)

    The Kano Model (Noriaki Kano, 1980s) prioritizes features "based on the degree to which they are likely to satisfy customers." Basic/threshold features are expected and taken for granted ("a car's turn signal"); performance/one-dimensional features deliver satisfaction in proportion to investment; excitement/attractive features create "dramatic customer delight" when present but "customers might not even miss them" when absent. Indifferent features customers "won't care about." A known dynamic: today's delighter becomes tomorrow's expected baseline.

  11. blackswanfarming.com ·cited· (T2)

    Weighted Shortest Job First sequences work by "dividing the estimated Cost of Delay by the estimated duration" — "the weighting that Don [Reinertsen] specifically recommends for product development is Cost of Delay" because "most organisations... are most interested in maximising their economic return." Putting duration in the denominator "optimises the return on investment by minimising the total Delay Cost incurred" and "has the benefit of encouraging the breakdown of work into smaller batches."

  12. blackswanfarming.com ·cited· (T2)

    Cost of Delay quantifies time's impact on value: "Cost of Delay is a way of communicating the impact of time on the outcomes we hope to achieve." Its power is conceptual — Reinertsen calls it "the golden key that unlocks many doors... an astonishing power to transform the mindset of a development organisation" — because it "combines urgency and value — two things that humans are not very good at distinguishing between," forcing teams to separate the genuinely time-critical from the merely valuable.

  13. productplan.com ·cited· (T2)

    Opportunity scoring is "an importance-versus-satisfaction analysis" traceable to "the outcome-driven-innovation (ODI) method created by business consultant Tony Ulwick in the 1990s." The opportunity algorithm is "Importance + (importance – satisfaction) = opportunity," and "the features or outcomes that represent your strongest opportunities will be those that receive the highest aggregate 'importance' and lowest aggregate 'satisfaction' scores" — i.e., it surfaces what customers care about most and are served worst on.

  14. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Tony Ulwick's Outcome-Driven Innovation starts from jobs-to-be-done: "People buy products and services to get jobs done," and "as people complete these jobs, they have certain measurable outcomes that they are attempting to achieve." ODI "attempts to identify jobs and outcomes that are either important but poorly served or unimportant but over-served," using "Importance + (Importance-Satisfaction) = Opportunity" — a formula that "gives twice as much weight to importance as to satisfaction" and segments markets by desired outcome rather than demographics.

  15. airfocus.com ·cited· (T2)

    MoSCoW sorts work into "four prioritization categories: Must-have, Should-have, Could-have, and Won't-have." Must-haves are "the minimum requirements... non-negotiable"; Should-haves "add substantial value" but aren't essential; Could-haves are "neat, interesting, or fun, but... [don't] necessarily serve any greater purpose"; Won't-haves are explicitly deferred. Developed by "Dai Clegg... consulting on software development at Oracle" in 1994, it "became popular in the agile project delivery framework DSDM."

  16. svpg.com ·cited· (T3)

    Marty Cagan argues product roadmaps should be simple, high-level paths from current state to product vision — not prioritized feature-request lists — and warns that feature-driven roadmaps skip the critical work of product discovery.

  17. svpg.com ·cited· (T3)

    Marty Cagan argues product roadmaps should be simple, high-level paths from current state to product vision — not prioritized feature-request lists — and warns that feature-driven roadmaps skip the critical work of product discovery.

  18. svpg.com ·cited· (T3)

    Marty Cagan argues product roadmaps should be simple, high-level paths from current state to product vision — not prioritized feature-request lists — and warns that feature-driven roadmaps skip the critical work of product discovery.

  19. blog.brianbalfour.com (T3)

    Everything's changed. Here's how to reach $100m at venture speed.

  20. amazon.com ·cited· (T4)

    Amazon audiobook listing for Measure What Matters by John Doerr, which covers the OKR goal-setting framework as adopted by Google and other leading organizations.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_product-roadmap-and-prioritization.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
